summary: Cantrill traces his journey from dismissing the ACM as irrelevant to practitioners to becoming an advocate for the organization after witnessing its transformation through ACM Queue and the revitalized Communications of the ACM. Initially viewing the ACM as lost in academic abstraction, he was won over by Queue's focus on practical engineering content. The merger of Queue into CACM's Practice section represents a broader organizational shift to include practitioners alongside academics, creating a publication that serves both communities.
key_points: ACM Queue proved that practitioner-focused content could succeed within the traditionally academic ACM
The old Communications of the ACM was so disconnected that even past ACM presidents admitted they couldn't read it
The new CACM bridges academic research and practical engineering, benefiting both communities
Software engineering as a profession lacks the formal professional responsibility expectations found in law, medicine, and traditional engineering
money_quotes: CACM was broadly viewed within the ACM as being woefully out of touch with both academic and practitioner alike, with one past president confessing that he himself couldn't stomach reading it – even when his name was on the masthead.

As professionals, I believe that we have a responsibility to our craft: to learn from our peers, to offer whatever we might have to teach, and to generally leave the profession better than we found it.

In other professions – in law, in medicine, and in more traditional engineering domains – this professional responsibility is indoctrinated to the point of expectation. But our discipline perhaps shows its youth in our ignorance of this kind of professional service.

The ACM now aspires to be the organization that represents all computer scientists – not just those who teach students, perform research and write papers, but also those of us who cut code, deliver product and deploy systems for a living.
The ACM has given us practitioners a new voice – but it is only good if we use it!
key_insight: Software engineering's maturation as a profession requires practitioners to engage with professional organizations, and the ACM's transformation shows that historically academic institutions can successfully embrace those who build production systems.
